critic_raw:
The hybrid CNN-RNN design for readmission prediction has a structural fairness problem in feature access. The CNN-RNN model processes raw temporal sequences of treatments and procedures, while the logistic regression baseline receives only aggregate summary features (counts, binary flags). The baseline is described as excluding raw sequences 'which logistic regression cannot process natively' — but this conflates baseline choice with fairness. A recurrent neural baseline or a gradient-boosted model on the same sequence features could receive the temporal data in an appropriate form. The current comparison therefore conflates architectural advantage with feature richness, making it impossible to attribute any performance gap to architecture rather than informational asymmetry.

The design uses AUPRC as the primary metric and explicitly argues it is appropriate for imbalanced data, but the choice is not well-defended for the stated operational goal. AUPRC is sensitive to the absolute number of positives and tends to favor models that assign high probabilities to the minority class indiscriminately. The secondary metric — sensitivity at 90% specificity — is operationally more aligned with the clinical decision problem described (resource-constrained intervention programs), yet it is relegated to a secondary role. The primary metric should be selected to match the deployment decision function, and here that appears to be a recall-at-fixed-specificity criterion.

The department-level readmission baseline is computed from training data and used as a feature for both models. While the description correctly notes this is fit on training data, the procedure introduces a form of target encoding leakage risk: the department-level readmission rate is a direct function of the outcome variable. Even when computed from training data only, this feature encodes mean outcome information and can cause overfitting on smaller departments where the estimate is noisy. The design does not apply any shrinkage or regularization to these target statistics, nor does it use cross-fitting (computing the statistic out-of-fold) within training to reduce bias.
defender_raw:
The hybrid CNN-RNN design for readmission prediction is a well-structured experiment that makes defensible architectural choices and implements robust methodological safeguards. The CNN-RNN combination is motivated by the problem structure: treatment sequences (procedures, medications) have both local patterns — drug combinations at a single visit — and temporal dependencies across visits, and a convolutional layer followed by an LSTM is a standard and effective architecture for exactly this structure. Criticizing this as complex for complexity's sake ignores that the architecture maps directly onto the data-generating process.

The baseline is genuinely fair. Logistic regression receives the same engineered features that it can actually process — including length-of-stay categories, admission history, and department-level baseline rates — and equivalent hyperparameter tuning via grid search. The claim that the comparison isolates architectural contribution from feature quality disparity is credible because the baseline is not deliberately weakened; it receives all information the hybrid model has except the raw sequences, which logistic regression cannot natively process. This is an honest counterfactual.

The choice of AUPRC as the primary metric is particularly well-justified for this problem. At 15-20% positive rate, AUROC can produce misleadingly optimistic impressions because it includes the large portion of the ROC curve where the threshold is set to catch almost all negatives. AUPRC focuses on the positive class — the patients who will actually be readmitted — and reflects the clinical reality that intervention programs are resource-constrained and cannot act on every high-risk flag. The additional calibration diagnostics via the Hosmer-Lemeshow test and calibration plots reflect genuine clinical deployment requirements: a model with good discrimination but poor calibration is dangerous when clinicians use its probability estimates for decision-making.

The confound controls are thoughtful. Department-level baseline readmission rates are included as features in both models, allowing both to adjust for case-mix heterogeneity. The sensitivity analysis by department on the test set is designed to detect exactly the failure mode where the hybrid model improves in some departments while degrading in others — a pattern that would indicate the apparent gain is distributional rather than generalizable.
all_issues_raised: Feature access asymmetry: CNN-RNN receives raw temporal sequences while logistic regression baseline is limited to summaries, confounding architectural and informational comparisons
No recurrent or sequence-capable baseline to isolate architectural contribution from feature richness
AUPRC selected as primary metric despite sensitivity-at-specificity being more aligned with the described deployment context
Department-level target encoding feature introduces leakage risk and potential overfitting on small departments without shrinkage or cross-fitting
CNN hyperparameters (filter counts, LSTM units, dense layers) are fully specified without any reported architecture search, suggesting undisclosed selection bias
all_issues_adjudicated: Feature access asymmetry: CNN-RNN receives raw temporal sequences while logistic regression baseline is limited to summaries, confounding architectural and informational comparisons
No recurrent or sequence-capable baseline to isolate architectural contribution from feature richness
Department-level target encoding feature introduces leakage risk and potential overfitting on small departments without shrinkage or cross-fitting
